summary: claude-cup ships a credential harvester that auto-runs from CLI startup and persists via Claude Code hooks
details: claude-cup@0.7.5 advertises itself as a 'Claude Cup contest visualizer' but ships research/config-audit.js (~45KB), a generic credential-stealer that recursively walks the user's home directory for.env files,.pem/.key/.pfx, shell history, and reads ~/.aws/credentials, ~/.ssh/*, ~/.npmrc, ~/.git-credentials, ~/.netrc, ~/.kube/config, ~/.docker/config.json, and ~/.claude/.credentials.json. On macOS it dumps the login keychain via `security dump-keychain`; on Windows it lists stored credentials via `cmdkey /list`; it copies Chrome/Edge cookie SQLite databases to read auth cookies. The harvester is wired into normal startup: src/cli.js runs `setTimeout(calibrate, 5000); setInterval(calibrate, 120_000)`, and mcp-server/src/calibrator.js invokes runConfigAudit. After collection, raw secrets are POSTed to validator endpoints — GitHub PAT to api.github.com/user with Authorization header, npm token to registry.npmjs.org/-/whoami (after writing a temp.npmrc), OpenAI key to api.openai.com/v1/models, Anthropic key to api.anthropic.com/v1/models, AWS access key+secret invoked via `aws sts get-caller-identity` with env-injected creds, plus GitLab/HuggingFace/Stripe/Google. The scan patterns, vault commands, and validator URLs are read live from a mutable manifest at https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Itaib24/Claude-/main/claude-jar/research/manifest.json (no commit pin), so the author can silently retarget the raw-token POSTs to any destination at any time without publishing a new npm version. mcp-server/src/fingerprint.js additionally sends a geo/host fingerprint to http://ip-api.com/json over plain HTTP. On first run, mcp-server/src/registration.js writes ~/.claude/settings.json to register SessionStart/PreToolUse/PostToolUse hooks pointing at ~/.claude-jar/mcp-server.mjs, so every future Claude Code action re-triggers the credential audit — and these hook entries persist after `npm uninstall claude-cup`. The README's claim that 'nothing is uploaded' and that raw credential values are 'never stored, transmitted, or logged' is materially false; the in-source comment 'Deep analysis uses a different module not published to npm' is also false because research/config-audit.js is declared in package.json `files` and the deep-analysis gate defaults to enabled whenever that file exists (which it always does in the published tarball).

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
